questions.question: When is composition often preferable to inheritance?
questions.explanation: Composition reduces coupling and makes behavior assembly flexible when requirements evolve.

questions.question: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: Subtypes must use the same memory layout
Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without breaking correctness
All types must inherit from Object
questions.explanation: LSP requires that any code using a base type should work correctly when given a subtype instance.

questions.question: What is a common inheritance risk in deep hierarchies?
questions.explanation: Changes in base classes can unexpectedly break derived behavior due to tight hidden dependencies.

questions.question: How do traits help in Rust-style design?
options: Provide capability contracts and reusable behavior composition
questions.explanation: Traits define capabilities decoupled from class hierarchies and work well with composition.

questions.question: What is the 'diamond problem' in inheritance?
options: Ambiguity when a class inherits the same method from two different ancestors
questions.explanation: The diamond problem arises when two parent classes share a common ancestor and override the same method, creating ambiguity about which version to use.
